one_line_takeaway: SemTaint shows that restricting LLM agent inference to static-analysis-identified vulnerability-relevant call edges and source/sink candidates (TICR + demand-driven flow-summary validation) can recover 65.43% of npm vulnerabilities CodeQL's taint-tracking otherwise misses entirely, using 94.5% fewer LLM calls than exhaustive resolution, and surfaced 4 confirmed novel vulnerabilities in real npm packages.
key_finding: On the 162-vulnerability held-out test set, SemTaint produced valid alerts for 106 vulnerabilities (65.43% recall) that CodeQL's baseline detected 0% of by construction (the dataset was filtered to exclude anything CodeQL already caught). Per-CWE recall ranged from 100% (CWE-74, 1/1) down to 0% for three low-count CWEs (CWE-829, CWE-918, CWE-327, each n=1), with larger categories such as CWE-22 (path traversal) at 82.14% (23/28) and CWE-400 (resource exhaustion) at 61.90% (26/42). On the 10-vulnerability validation set, SemTaint found 9/10, generating 30 alerts with 6 false positives (80% precision). The ablation study (Table 4, over all 115 vulnerabilities detected across both datasets) attributed 84 detections (73.0%) to custom source/sink specification alone (ruleset R3, using CodeQL's existing call graph), and 31 (27.0%) required call graph repair; of those 31, only 8 were reachable with CodeQL's default sources/sinks plus enhanced call graph (R2), meaning 23 needed both custom sources/sinks and call graph repair jointly. On efficiency (RQ2), TICR reduced the candidate unresolved-call set from 94,909 to 10,184 (89.2% reduction) across the 162 test packages, and after auto-assigning 4,989 as third-party via static queries, only 5,195 calls required agent resolution -- a 94.5% overall reduction in LLM calls (average per-package agent calls dropped from 585.9 to 32.1). Demand-driven flow-summary validation reduced third-party edge analysis by 93.2% (6,639 candidates down to 449 actually analyzed). For RQ4, across 10 manually validated OSS packages, SemTaint produced 34 alerts, of which 13 were confirmed true positives corresponding to 4 distinct novel vulnerabilities (one per package) spanning command injection (CWE-78), path traversal (CWE-22), and prototype pollution (CWE-915); the remaining 21 alerts split into 10 cases of correct taint paths blocked by sanitization that CodeQL's sanitizer models missed, and 11 cases representing intended package functionality (e.g., a CLI wrapper intentionally forwarding input to shell execution) rather than a vulnerability.
core_contribution: The paper presents SemTaint, a static-led, LLM-augmented vulnerability analysis system for JavaScript/npm that treats CodeQL's static analysis as the source of truth and invokes LLM agents only at specific points where static reasoning under-approximates. SemTaint uses three specialized LLM agents built on a shared read-only code-exploration toolkit (view_src, find_string, view_dir): (1) a Source/Sink Agent that proposes CWE-specific, package-specific sources and sinks bound to CodeQL data-flow (QL) nodes; (2) a CallGraph Agent that resolves unresolved call edges using a novel algorithm, Taint-Informed Callee Resolution (TICR), which restricts LLM queries to unresolved calls lying on potential vulnerability paths for the CWE of interest, producing both first-party call edges and conservative 'candidate flow summary' placeholders for third-party (npm dependency) calls; and (3) a Flow Summary Agent that validates only those candidate third-party flow summaries that actually appear in reported vulnerability paths, determining whether the corresponding library API sanitizes or propagates taint. The resulting taint specification (sources, sinks, call edges, flow summaries) is fed back into CodeQL as external predicates to complete vulnerability analysis. The authors integrate this with CodeQL v2.21.2 and evaluate it against a filtered subset of Brito et al.'s npm vulnerability dataset, plus a newly constructed set of 102 popular OSS npm packages for novel-vulnerability discovery.
threat_model: The paper does not present an adversarial threat model in the traditional sense (attacker capabilities/goals); it is a vulnerability-detection tool paper. The implicit 'threat' addressed is that static taint analysis for JavaScript (via CodeQL) systematically misses real vulnerabilities because (a) JavaScript's dynamic features (runtime object composition, computed property dispatch, closures, prototype-based dispatch, eval) break call-graph construction, and (b) npm packages transitively depend on ~80 libraries whose taint-relevant behavior is excluded from analysis by default. The paper's own motivating example (Section 2, GHSA-hpr5-wp7c-hh5q, a stored/reflected XSS in the 320-line mrk.js markdown parser) walks through three concrete breaks in CodeQL's static reasoning: (1) an IIFE/factory pattern hiding the true taint-source entry point, (2) enumeration-based dispatch via Object.assign/Object.entries obscuring which pattern-matching function executes, and (3) computed-property dispatch (mrk.htmlify[token.name]) obscuring the sink. The vulnerability classes targeted are the 37 CWEs for which CodeQL supports taint-tracking queries (e.g., CWE-79 XSS, CWE-78 command injection, CWE-22 path traversal, CWE-89 SQL injection, CWE-915 prototype pollution, CWE-400 resource exhaustion, CWE-94 code injection).
limitations: The authors' own Threats to Validity section states: (1) alert classification relied on heuristic matching against Brito et al.'s annotated source/sink pairs, with 'extended path' and 'equivalent flow' categories requiring manual two-reviewer verification, which may miss valid alerts differing structurally from ground-truth annotations and inherits any labeling errors in the underlying dataset; (2) candidate flow summaries model taint only as returning through the invocation site, missing cases where third-party libraries propagate taint via callbacks -- full review of every such call would substantially raise inference cost; (3) TICR's taint-reachability analysis can miss security-relevant calls when intra-procedural gaps sever the path before reaching an unresolved call, only partially mitigated by 'extended flow semantics' and an exhaustive-mode fallback; (4) external validity is limited to JavaScript/npm -- extension to other languages needs reimplementation of the CodeQL integration layer even though the agent architecture is claimed to be language-agnostic in principle; (5) the 1M-token limit excluded 21 packages from the known-vulnerability dataset, and coverage is limited to the 37 CWEs CodeQL's taint-tracking queries support; (6) the Brito et al. dataset only covers vulnerabilities disclosed through June 2021, so generalization to current JavaScript/npm practices is uncertain, and the OSS dataset's filter for packages with prior GHSA records may bias toward already-scrutinized packages; (7) intra-procedural data-flow gaps and CodeQL's sanitizer over-approximation are explicitly out of scope and unaddressed. Additionally, false positives in the 162-vulnerability testing dataset were not investigated by the authors (stated focus was recall only), so testing-set precision is not reported, unlike the 80% precision reported for the small 10-item validation set.

datasets_used: Brito et al. npm vulnerability dataset (957 manually verified vulnerabilities from npm security advisories through June 2021), filtered via 6 criteria to 172 vulnerabilities (10 validation + 162 test, spanning 17 CWEs)

OSS Dataset: 102 popular npm packages constructed from the top 5,000 Libraries.io SourceRank-ranked packages, filtered by dependents/stars/GHSA history/CWE coverage; 10 of these received full manual PoC validation for RQ4
quantitative_results: Testing set recall: 106/162 = 65.43% vulnerabilities detected (vs. CodeQL baseline 0% on this filtered set)
Validation set: 9/10 vulnerabilities found, 30 alerts, 6 false positives, 80% precision

Ablation attribution (115 total detections across both datasets): 84 (73.0%) via custom source/sink alone (R3); 31 (27.0%) required call graph repair; of those 31, only 8 detectable via default sources/sinks + enhanced call graph (R2), 23 required both enhancements jointly

TICR reduced candidate unresolved calls from 94,909 to 10,184 (89.2% reduction); after auto-assigning 4,989 third-party via static queries, only 5,195 required agent resolution, a 94.5% overall reduction in LLM calls; average per-package agent calls dropped from 585.9 to 32.1
Demand-driven flow summary validation reduced third-party edge analysis 93.2% (6,639 candidates to 449 analyzed)

RQ4 novel vulnerability discovery: 34 alerts across 10 manually validated OSS packages; 13 confirmed TPs corresponding to 4 distinct novel vulnerabilities (CWE-78, CWE-22, CWE-915) in 4 different packages; 10 alerts were sanitized-but-flagged (CodeQL sanitizer gaps), 11 were intended functionality
Re-running CodeQL baseline with accessPathLimit/fieldFlowBranchLimit raised from 2 to 5000 found zero additional vulnerabilities, ruling out budget-limit confounds
baselines_compared: CodeQL v2.21.2 baseline taint-tracking queries (unmodified) -- primary baseline, R1 in ablation, 0% recall on the filtered test/validation sets by construction
Model-choice comparison (Table 5): GPT-5/GPT-5-mini vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro/Gemini-2.5-Flash agent configurations, GPT-based configurations chosen for higher recall
Ablation rulesets R2-R7 (combinations of custom sources/sinks, enhanced call graph, and sanitization barrier toggling) used as internal component-attribution baselines rather than external tools

Related work discussed but NOT directly re-run as an experimental baseline in this paper: IRIS and QLPro (LLM-inferred sources/sinks for Java + CodeQL, relying on the static call graph, stateless/batched LLM classification), CALLME and GRAPHIA (learning-based JavaScript call graph repair), and TASER (dynamic taint-specification extraction for JS libraries via test-suite execution) -- these are cited as prior work with limitations SemTaint addresses, not as tools benchmarked head-to-head in this paper's experiments
